speaker: interviewer
text: OK. At the end of the last reel, you were talking about your grandparents and how they were taken to Auschwitz too. But before we begin, before we get to 1939 and what happened, I was wondering if you could tell me a little bit more. Could you describe what Pesach was like at your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pesach was a very special holiday in our house because we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we should fall asleep. The services were conducted and the songs were sang such a long time that most-- my younger brother and I, we always fell asleep. But it was a very cheerful type of a holiday, with many songs and asking the four questions, which I always volunteered to be the first one to ask the-- the four questions, which are called the [YIDDISH]. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was a very festive holiday in our house. My mother used to work quite a bit to cook their meals and to prepare the dishes to make them kosher for Pesach, and boil them in water in big pots. And it was quite a-- a busy time in-- in our home. But it was also a very special time, a lot of anticipation of Pesach, of the Seder. And it was a-- It was a long holiday because we used to have two Seders, and then have the [HEBREW]. So it was a festive as well as a religious holiday. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were sitting at the table and all I remember, that took a long time for the Seders to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to complete the Seders. It used to be very late at night. So I know that my younger brother definitely, and I myself, fell sleep most of the time at the end. We never finished it. So-- but it was a very important holiday [INAUDIBLE] in my home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Since you lived in an apartment building with all your relatives, how did you cele-- did you each individually celebrate it, or did you all together at one-- one big table? How did you-- with your uncles and your aunts and your cousins and your grandparents, the Enochs?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my-- well, my grandfather was a widower. So he would eat a Pesach. I remember he used to come to us quite often. And he would go to his daughter's, because we were-- we had-- we lived in the building. Two of my aunts-- other aunts lived in the building, and his son.

speaker: interviewer
text: So most of them had their own Seder. But my grandfather used to always visit. And one night he would spend with us, another night he spend with somebody else. But it-- this holiday I remember quite well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Unlike other holidays which were, like Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, that was-- where we spent a lot of-- a long time in the temple listening to the-- to the cousin and the rabbi speaking and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] waiting for any-- for the [NON-ENGLISH] to [INAUDIBLE] so that we-- the services would be over. And we had a temple that was-- the men were sitting on the first floor, and the women were sitting on the second floor. It was like a round-- round dome with balconies. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on Yom Kippur, many people would faint and-- because of the fasting. It was a very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] everyone was at awe, to, and-- and especially on Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur. It was like [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a-- I don't know how to describe it, but it was like [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a special atmosphere. Like in Hebrew they would say like [HEBREW], [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] a solemn occasion. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then we also had very festive and less religious holidays, like Purim, and-- which we used to get-- we used to go and make noises in the temple with the [INAUDIBLE] and we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was too small, but my brothers and my father used to get the [NON-ENGLISH], dancing with the Torah. And-- and-- and Hanukkah was probably the most enjoyable because we had candles, lit candles every night, and-- and drank. And we sang the various songs about Hanukkah and about the destruction of the Temple and the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the, also, the [INAUDIBLE] how the Miracle of the Lights, how there's a song that describes it, how the candles were burning for eight days without-- without-- actually, not the candles, the oil was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I remember these--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the song? Do you remember the song?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it go?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SINGING IN HEBREW] We sang various songs. This one I remember the best.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. That was beautiful. You know, you said that, since you had such an important family in Oswiecim, in the-- what you didn't-- what I'd like to know is, in terms of Jewish and non-Jewish, how big a town was Oswiecim? And percentage wise, how many Jews do you think-- and did you all live in the same area, or were you kind of spread out? How did that work, since Auschwitz is so important.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say that in my father-- grandfather's building, there mainly was-- there was Jewish people, mainly, living there. I don't remember. We had a caretaker which was non-Jewish. And he had his own apartment, because of-- he was the custodian, the caretaker. And also he used to come around on Shabbat and high holidays, and turn on and off the candles, the lights, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and perform some work that was not allowed because of the Shabbat, of the Sabbath. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] The family was important in Oswiecim because it was a-- an old, established family. I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my grandmother [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] came from-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] her name was-- maiden name was Van [? Plasner. ?] She-- they were old Austrian Jews with a title. So she was a contessa, count-- a countess. Van [? Plasner, ?] that was her maiden name. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this is the same person that I describe where people-- you should tell me how they-- they say we need more people like this now to help the poor. And your grandmother was such a terrific person that she made sure that the poor people had food and-- and were able to keep warm. And that was her. You should take the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] help, you know, the service people-- the servants, and-- and help distribute foods and clothes and wood, and mainly a lot of food.

speaker: interviewer
text: How unusual for a Jewish family to have a title. How did your family get that title?

speaker: interviewee
text: My understanding is that the [GERMAN], [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the title was-- they were-- they were from Vienna. And they-- her father, my grandmother's father, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was a professor. And he wrote a-- some special type of [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] books.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was rewarded with a title, which was very rare because, in-- especially in Europe, for Jews. But I understand that Austria was the very first one to recognize that, and that the Jews lived like there was no difference between-- supposedly, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because my father and my mother, they spoke German quite well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they always used to say how wonderful it was, you know, when-- because there was an expression that the Germans and the Austrians used to use. Maybe in those days they used to say, 'Leben und leben lassen.' Live and let live. But this really was not-- I did not experience that. I experienced just the opposite.

speaker: interviewer
text: I asked you, but I think we got waylaid a little bit. In terms of Jewish and non-Jewish in Oswiecim, what--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry, right. I don't know exactly. We had very little to do with the non-Jewish people. And we really didn't have any neighbors that would live next door. So all I know, that we know that-- we knew that the antisemitism was always present, especially on Easter time. We used to close the gate and put the [NON-ENGLISH] on the door, close the-- because they would throw stones. And Christmas was probably the worst time because they-- the Pollacks truly believed that the Jews killed Christ, they-- that the Jews killed their God. And the antisemitism in Poland was inbred in-- at-- in home already. When the chil-- they talked about it, about the dirty Jews at home before the children even started school. I do remember an incident where [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- where a group of children, when I was in school, they-- they were-- they was-- they were-- they got a hold of me and they-- I was hit with a stone in my head. And I came home all bloody. My mother almost fainted. But-- but I knew exactly which guy did it. And there was maybe four or five of them together. And I said, I'm not going to stand for it. So I-- I did seek this guy out separately. So it was a different situation. And he really-- he would not participate anymore because, one on one, they were not that brave.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was definite antisemitism. But I would say that probably the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] less-- where people had lived among the Poles, maybe experienced it more so than we did. I-- The only time that I really came in contact with Polish people were because of the public school. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: My teacher, I remember was a major in the Polish army. And all my-- all my siblings went to the same school. So the parents' conference, they used to go and find out how-- and my-- I can't think of his name, but he would say, [POLISH]. It means, Mrs. Bader, you should only have this much pleasure from the rest of your children, because [INAUDIBLE] is doing a terrific job. You know, so-- I used to I used to work at it. And I-- and my grandfather was-- when we-- when we used to get our report cards, he would-- I would get the biggest amount of the-- whatever. Someone would get maybe a dollar, maybe I would get $2 because they all knew that I would bring the best report card. And he would sit in the the main-- inside the square of our building, and the kids would pass by him, and he would reward them for-- for getting-- but he would give each one something, regardless.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, I just am curious. You said that when you went to seek out that-- the boy who had hit you in the head with the stone, what did you do to him that you-- that he stopped?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I told him that I'm going to beat the hell out of him, and I did, yes. And I-- and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and since then, he would not participate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a radio in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a radio towards the year-- I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was the end of-- before the Germans came. I remember having a radio. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you listen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we-- I did. And we also had, besides the radio, we had a micro-- a gramophone. And I used to always, especially during the war where our parents would not-- didn't let the kids go out at night, so friends and relatives would come to our apartment. And I-- and I would be given five pennies to keep it-- to-- I would crawl under the table and keep the gramophone-- I would wind it up constantly and replace-- and change the plates, the-- the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] record. Right, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of music did you like to listen?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of-- because they danced and they-- so a lot of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] variety of music, I would say, a lot of the kind of music you can dance to. And some of it was even classical music, because we had a-- I remember the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one tenor whose name was Yom Kippur. He was quite-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the young people liked to listen to it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I used to-- I was in charge of the microphone-- of the gramophone. And we had a little radio which we would also listen to.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said people danced. Did you have big band-- like, did you have American music, or did you have kind of Polish music, or Jewish music?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably more Jewish and whatever they were dancing in those days, maybe tango and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] waltz or polka.

speaker: interviewer
text: But nothing like Benny Goodman or Glenn Miller?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No, that I experienced after. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: From the radio, I know you were so young, but were you-- did you ever hear anything on the radio about what was happening in Germany, about what Hitler was saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we heard on the radio. We also-- it was written in the papers as well as-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and we were concerned about it. I remember my father talking about that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they felt that, you know, being who they are and where they are, that maybe nothing would happen to them. That was a big mistake. That-- because I remember when they were talking about these things and-- and even I-- I used to read the papers quite a bit, whatever paper we had in Oswiecim, because I was fascinated prior to that how, for so many years, the Japanese and the Chinese were fighting, you know, and so many people were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I couldn't grasp that. You know, why should they, you know, fight? This went on for such a long time. So I was always interested to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] find out what's happening in the world. But I was, at that same time, when the Pole-- when the Germans were arming themselves and the-- and the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they were-- they demanded Sudentengow and they-- then they-- the [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they took back.

speaker: interviewee
text: All this was taking place, and the Poles in the parliament were talking about the shechita. I don't know if you-- the [HEBREW] means how you slaughter animal, like for food, you know. And they did not-- they were discussing it that the Jews should not-- you cannot shoot the animal. It has to be slaughtered by a shochet. And that's what they were occupied with. Because not too long after that, I was listening to the radio where they were-- where the Germans were getting ready to occupy Poland. And to-- listening to the radio was like a comedy, that they had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] passwords like [POLISH]. And then we could see-- they had little tanks. We could see the tanks. They were totally disorganized. And the horses pulling the-- the-- the cannons. But they mainly, the little tanks, like maybe one or two man tank, probably one man tank. And when they would say [POLISH], they would go one direction. When they would say [POLISH], they would say-- go in the other direction. It was total chaos.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were never prepared for what really happened [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] because they were busy with things that didn't make sense at all. You know? But I do remember that very well because this was the topic, you know, that the Jews would not eat the meat because it's not kosher. It has to be done in a very ritual way, slaughtered by a shochet which also blessed the food as well, the meat and specially prepared. In that summer of 1939, in Sept-- June, July, August of 1939, you were 12, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was 12 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything in particular before the actual invasion of Poland, or did you-- that summer, did you carry on as you had always?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was anticipation and worries and concern, and a lot of talk about it. But unfortunately no action. No one took any action. Maybe some people did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do that summer? Do you remember where you were, what you were doing that summer of 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: The summer of 1939, I remember that the-- Marshal Pilsudski died and the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think  it was 1939. And they elected a new marshal, which was a protege to-- his name was Smigly-Rydz-- took the place. And they were singing songs about how they're going to fight the Germans. And they would take an example from the Smigly-Rydz, they would-- and that they are ready for them, but they were-- really didn't. The Polish Army was always terrific when it came to prepare a parade because they had those white horses, [INAUDIBLE], pulling the cannons. But when the German came, the white horses were muddy and couldn't get a-- pull the cannons out of the mud.

speaker: interviewer
text: So neither the Poles, especially the Jews, never expected what really happened, because they-- they thought that maybe-- Adolf Hitler always said after he-- Sudentengow, when [INAUDIBLE] that he will not-- that's all he wants. And-- but Austria is part of Germany anyways. So-- But you yourself, that summer. I want to know about you. Not what happened politically, but you. Was there anything special that summer that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think it was any different. And-- maybe there was more participa-- anticipation of something that is-- that will be happening, of the war, that the war will come. They did not know exactly when. But life was normal as always, with the exception of this concern.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where you were when the invasion occurred?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the invasion occurred, we were-- I was-- yes, I remember. When the invasion occurred, we had-- there was some-- excuse me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were some wagons with troops, Polish troops, that pulled in into-- into our building, to the square of my grandfather's building. And they locked the gate. And they took off the Polish uniform, and underneath there was a German uniform. That's how well they-- I remember this, and I was-- and I saw it with my own eyes. The Poles never had a chance because they were infiltrated. Because the area where I was born, that was Silesia, and it was always either Poland or German. The language was going back and forth. So they were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were Germans that could speak Polish as well as the German language. And-- and I was amazed when I saw what was happening in a-- in a few hours. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The city was occupied by the Germans. I don't remember any bombing or destruction or any kind in Oswiecim. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but I was-- we locked the door to the apartment, and we stayed inside, and-- waiting to see what will happen next.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did happen next?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened next is they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] occupied the city real quick. And about a week later, or two weeks later, they changed the name from-- the main square to Adolf Hitler Square. And they made us use arm bands with the Star of David, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a yellow band. And then they changed this. And later on it was changed where you had to sew up a permanent yellow star and said Jude.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop here and change the reel.
